hazard.summary: Agentgateway currently requires Retry-After or x-ratelimit-reset headers to trigger 429-based failover. Vertex AI returns HTTP 429 with no retry/reset headers but does provide semantic body signal (`status: RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED`, `message: Resource exhausted. Please try again later.`). Because headerless 429s are not classified from body semantics, failover to lower-priority groups does not trigger, leaving requests pinned to exhausted backends and increasing the chance of avoidable client-visible 429s.
signals: HTTP 429 from Vertex AI with no Retry-After header
HTTP 429 from Vertex AI with no x-ratelimit-reset header
Body contains message: Resource exhausted. Please try again later.
Failover documentation requires 429 plus proper rate-limit headers to trigger failover
Collaborator workaround suggests brief backend eviction on any 429 rather than body-aware classification

constraints: Header-absent 429 responses must still be classifiable when the response body carries semantic exhaustion signal.
Failover must not depend exclusively on Retry-After or reset headers when providers omit them.
Classification input should include response body semantics when headers are absent or incomplete.
Retry or eviction workarounds are not substitutes for correct failover classification.
When alternate providers or regions exist, exhausted backends should not remain the active routing target solely because retry headers are missing.
verification: Configure a priority group with multiple Vertex AI backends and a lower-priority fallback group.
Trigger a Vertex AI HTTP 429 with body status RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED and no Retry-After or x-ratelimit-reset headers.
Verify current behavior does not fail over to the lower-priority group.
Add body-aware 429 classification or opt-in headerless failover support.
Verify exhausted Vertex AI backends are routed away from when semantic body signal indicates failover-worthy exhaustion.
Verify transient headerless 429s can still take a short-eviction / retry path when body semantics indicate temporary pressure rather than sustained exhaustion.
notes: This receipt captures a classification blind spot at the failover boundary: semantic 429 handling is currently gated on retry/reset headers that some providers do not emit. In this case, Vertex AI provides the only usable classification signal in the response body. The collaborator-proposed workaround (`unhealthyCondition: response.code == 429` with 10-second eviction) may improve availability operationally, but it is still a retry / eviction workaround rather than a classification fix. The economic consequence is directional but not yet numerically bounded: healthy alternates may be skipped, client-visible 429s may increase, and retries or short evictions may waste time while traffic remains concentrated on exhausted backends.

constraints: headerless 429s must be classifiable from body semantics when headers are absent
failover cannot depend exclusively on Retry-After or reset headers
eviction workaround is not equivalent to classification
anti_patterns: treating missing retry headers as missing failover signal
requiring Retry-After to trigger all 429 failover behavior
using temporary eviction as a substitute for semantic classification
